note:
Completed items roll off revision_plan.json into here once their details no longer inform current work. Calendar history is preserved; the viewer's History section reads this file too.

log: the work record. Each entry is a `now` banner retired from revision_plan.json when the next one replaced it, timestamps intact, newest first. Nothing here is ever deleted or edited - it is what was true on the day it was written. The plan's `now` holds exactly one entry, which is why this file exists.

log.text: Stage 2 complete and the pane exists: MHALL plays the Harpington Padlington on hardware, and rev-ui-kit can scroll and zoom a region the application paints. Next: ui-06, MHALL visible on the piano roll.

Plan file reconstructed. An accidental `git checkout` on the tracked doc/revision_plan.json (while fault-testing the misc-05 checker) discarded its uncommitted edits, reverting it to commit 213481e. Rebuilt from the committed base: the midi arc (midi-01/02/03) and ui-06 marked complete with restored notes; eng-08 and misc-05 restored verbatim from the session record; dsp-04 and midi-04 rebuilt from the discussion (marked RECONSTRUCTED, terser than the originals). Nothing but this one file was affected; all code, proposals and other docs were intact.

dsp-02 complete: the PADsynth bake, in Rust, band-limited by construction. The geometry is the result worth keeping - half-octave table spacing plus a band limit at Nyquist/r_max means no partial can cross Nyquist at any playback rate, so fold-back is absent rather than attenuated. The two decisions are complements: the spacing is what makes the strict limit cost nothing. Discussion arrived there by being wrong first - the fold floor is SR*(1 - r_max/2) = 14.1 kHz at octave spacing, not the 17 kHz I claimed, which put it inside ordinary hearing rather than exceptional hearing. R-720 records why conformance is measured to Nyquist: a nonlinear reproduction chain translates inharmonic ultrasonic content downward as inharmonic, while harmonic content translates as harmonic and fuses. And the eng-07 debt is paid - rev-testkit can listen to pitch, and 37 notes assert it. What remains is measured and named: resampling images at -27.6 dB worst, which the reference instrument shares and only oversampled reading removes.

STAGE 2 COMPLETE: MHALL sounds, from stored material, on hardware. The engine track is done through eng-07 - the seam (eng-01), cpal bring-up and first tone (eng-03), the node runtime (eng-02), AudioParam automation (eng-04), the Padlington voice (eng-05), the schedule compiler and rev-sched (eng-06), and the join (eng-07). core-03 closed scoped to flat realization. 331 tests green. The lesson of this session, stated plainly: the two defects that mattered were heard, not measured. Pitch was wrong for a whole run and every test passed - bit-identity passes when wrong is reproducible, and nothing we had written listens to pitch. The same lesson ui-03 taught about state you cannot see. NEXT: dsp-02 (the PADsynth bake, which needs an FFT and should bring a spectral test that measures a fundamental), then dsp-03 certifies the voice. Also open: the stealing/determinism gap in eng-07's ignored test; nesting (core-03 finding, R-407/R-422) with the elaborated-MHALL fixture designed for it; core-04 to close stage 1; ui-04/05/06 (Control Bar to engine, log viewer, read-only roll on a continuous log-frequency axis per R-941); misc-03.

REVISION MAKES SOUND. eng-01 approved (21 decisions, no carve-outs) and archived to doc/completed/; eng-03 implemented and at verifying. The seam is four channels in three mechanisms: command ring and observation ring and return ring (rtrb SPSC), plus a hand-written position seqlock the counter reads. Engine::process is driver-agnostic - a cpal device driver and an offline driver call the same core - so CI runs the whole real-time discipline under an allocation guard on machines with no sound card, and R-1402's render-twice bit-identity gate works already. The governing law is R-312: below the engine boundary there is only physics, so the engine takes frequencies and never note numbers and tuning-awareness is structural. rev-engine depends on cpal and rtrb and nothing else - not rev-core, not rev-store, not rev-log. New crate rev-log writes one forever SQLite file (session as a column, WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, pruned at ~1 MB) with stderr echo in debug; every UI action and engine event now leaves a prose record. Verified on hardware: LG TV over HDMI/WASAPI, 48 kHz, 0 xruns, worst callback 68 us. Press Play in rev-app and you hear a tone while the counter follows the engine's own clock. Next: eng-02 (node API checkpoint) toward the PadSynth harpsichord/clavinet port, core-03/core-04 to close stage 1, then eng-06's schedule compiler which owes the chunk payload eng-01 deliberately deferred. Also open: ui-05 (log viewer), misc-03 (xtask license), core-01's content identity (R-1412).

ui-03 is at verifying: Revision has a Control Bar. It is described as data and rendered through a derived skin onto a CPU paint list - transport cluster, mode toggles, record-mode pop-up, a counter editable while playing, tempo, shuttle and eight locators, all operable. 194 tests green. The session's lesson is recorded in the plan and the standard: every defect that mattered was 'the state exists and you cannot see it', and every one was found by operating the thing rather than by a test. That is now backed by an authority - R-939 makes the Macintosh Human Interface Guidelines (1992) the default for interaction behaviour where our requirements are silent, inventoried with chapter citations in revision_hig_inventory.md, with the departures we make deliberately named in R-940. Next target: AUDIO OUTPUT. That means eng-01, the engine-app interface checkpoint - the most consequential seam in the project, and the thing ui-04 waits on. It must carry the session-keyed engine decision from the multi-document discussion. Also open: core-03/core-04 close stage 1; core-01 owes content identity (R-1412) before project files exist in the wild; misc-03 (xtask license) now has real assets to audit.
